questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient rates their pain as a 10/10 following surgery.  They are currently receiving morphine sulfate via patient-controlled analgesia (PCA).  What is the appropriate action?

choices.text: Tell the patient to wait until the next dose of medication.
Assess the patient for other potential causes of pain.
Increase the lockout interval on the PCA pump.
Immediately administer a bolus dose of morphine.

questions.rationale: A high pain score despite PCA use indicates a need to reassess the pain management plan. Exploring other factors (e.g., surgical site, anxiety) is crucial.  Simply increasing the dose without further assessment is risky.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with type 2 diabetes is experiencing hyperglycemia.  What is an important nursing assessment?

choices.text: Assess for ketones in the urine.

questions.rationale: All assessments are important in managing hyperglycemia. Dehydration and ketoacidosis can be complications.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Upon admission, a patient reports taking several medications at home.  What is the best way to ensure medication safety?

choices.text: Compare the patient's report to their medication list.
Contact the patient's pharmacy to verify medication list.

questions.rationale: All methods help ensure accurate medication reconciliation.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse just completed wound care for a patient with a methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infection. What should the nurse do next?

choices.text: Remove gloves and gown before leaving the room.
Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water.
Dispose of all materials in a regular trash can.
Document the procedure in the patient's chart.

questions.rationale: Thorough handwashing after contact with an MRSA infection is crucial to prevent transmission.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving oxygen therapy via nasal cannula. Which action should the nurse take to ensure patient safety?

choices.text: Place the oxygen tank near a heat source.
Check the oxygen flow rate regularly.
Allow the patient to smoke while receiving oxygen.
Use petroleum jelly to lubricate the cannula.

questions.rationale: Regular monitoring of the oxygen flow rate ensures the patient receives the prescribed amount of oxygen.  The other options are unsafe practices.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A client with HIV is experiencing opportunistic infections. What is the priority nursing intervention?
questions.rationale: Opportunistic infections in HIV are life-threatening; strict infection control (C) is the priority to prevent further infection. While A, B, and D are important, infection control is paramount in this situation.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task is MOST appropriate for a licensed practical nurse (LPN) to delegate to a certified nursing assistant (CNA)?

choices.text: Monitoring vital signs on a stable patient.
Educating a patient about a new medication.

questions.rationale: Monitoring vital signs on a stable patient (C) is within the scope of practice for a CNA and can be safely delegated by an LPN.  A, B, and D require the skills and judgment of a licensed nurse.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which precautions are necessary for a patient with methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA)? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: MRSA is spread through direct contact. Contact precautions (A), including gown and gloves (D), and a private room (F) are necessary.  Droplet (B) and airborne (C) precautions are not indicated for MRSA.  Mask and eye protection (E) are not routinely used for contact precautions unless there is a risk of splashing body fluids.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which type of isolation precaution is appropriate for a patient with tuberculosis?
questions.rationale: Tuberculosis is transmitted through airborne droplets.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which patient should the nurse assess FIRST?

choices.text: A patient with a new diagnosis of type 2 diabetes mellitus requiring education.
A patient who is 2 days post-op and reports incisional pain.
A patient with shortness of breath and oxygen saturation of 88%.
A patient who needs assistance with ambulation.

questions.rationale: The patient with shortness of breath and low oxygen saturation (C) has the most immediate and potentially life-threatening condition requiring immediate attention.  The other patients (A, B, D) require attention but not as urgently as the patient experiencing respiratory distress.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient rates their pain as a 9/10 after surgery.  What is the nurse's best action?

choices.text: Reassess the patient's pain in 30 minutes.
Apply a warm compress to the surgical site.

questions.rationale: A severe pain score of 9/10 warrants immediate intervention.  Administering prescribed analgesics (A) is the priority. Reassessment (B) is important, but analgesics should be given first. Warm compresses (C) and relaxation techniques (D) can be helpful but are not the immediate priority for severe pain.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which actions are necessary to prevent falls in a hospitalized patient? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Keep the bed in the low position.
Place the call light within reach.
Educate the patient about fall prevention.

questions.rationale: Fall prevention requires a comprehensive approach. Assessing fall risk (A) identifies individuals needing extra precautions. Keeping the bed low (B) reduces the height of a fall.  The call light (C) allows for timely assistance.  Bed rails (D) can provide additional stability, but should not be used if they pose a risk to the patient.  Educating the patient (F) empowers them to participate in their own safety.  Administering sedatives (E) increases the fall risk.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task is MOST appropriate for the RN to delegate to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?

choices.text: Monitoring a patient's neurological status post-stroke.
Changing a sterile dressing on a surgical wound.

questions.rationale: LPNs can perform tasks such as changing sterile dressings (D) under the supervision of an RN.  Administering IV medications (A), developing care plans (B), and monitoring complex neurological status (C) require the advanced skills and judgment of an RN.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A newly hired nurse is consistently late for work and makes frequent charting errors. Which action should the nurse manager take first?

choices.text: Document the issues and submit a performance improvement plan (PIP).
Report the nurse to the state board of nursing.

questions.rationale: Progressive discipline is essential.  Verbal counseling and coaching provide an opportunity for the nurse to improve before more formal actions are taken.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving oxygen therapy via nasal cannula. Which safety precaution is essential?

choices.text: Keep flammable materials away from the patient.
Ensure the patient's oxygen saturation is above 95%.
Monitor the patient for signs of hypoxemia.

questions.rationale: Oxygen is flammable, and precautions must be taken to prevent fire hazards.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with chronic back pain reports increased pain despite taking prescribed analgesics. What non-pharmacological pain management strategies should the nurse suggest?
questions.rationale: Multiple non-pharmacological methods can be used to manage chronic pain.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action best demonstrates the principles of infection control in a community setting?

choices.text: Wearing a mask while visiting a patient with influenza.
Properly disposing of sharps in designated containers.
Using gloves when handling bodily fluids.

questions.rationale: Frequent and thorough handwashing (C) is the most fundamental and effective infection control measure in all settings, including community health.  While the other options are also important, handwashing is the cornerstone of preventing the spread of infections.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task is MOST appropriate for the RN to delegate to a Licensed Practical Nurse (LPN)?

choices.text: Monitoring a patient's response to medication.

questions.rationale: LPNs can monitor a patient's response to medication (D) and report changes to the RN.  Administering IV medications (A), assessing a new patient (B), and developing a care plan (C) are within the RN's scope of practice.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with suspected tuberculosis (TB) is admitted. Which precautions are necessary?
questions.rationale: TB requires airborne precautions (D) to prevent the spread of airborne pathogens.  Standard precautions (A) are always necessary to prevent transmission of microorganisms from all patients.  Contact (B), droplet (C), and protective isolation (E) are not indicated in TB cases.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse is caring for four patients: one with acute respiratory distress, one with chest pain, one with a new-onset seizure, and one with post-surgical pain. Which patient requires the nurse's immediate attention?

choices.text: The patient with acute respiratory distress.
The patient with a new-onset seizure.

questions.rationale: Acute respiratory distress (A) is a life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention to prevent respiratory failure. While the other patients need care, the patient with acute respiratory distress requires the most immediate attention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which precautions are necessary when caring for a patient with Clostridium difficile (C. diff)?

choices.text: Use of soap and water for hand hygiene

questions.rationale: C. difficile is spread through contact with spores.  Contact precautions are essential.  Soap and water are necessary to remove the spores, alcohol-based hand rubs are not effective.  Gown and gloves are part of contact precautions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse observes a colleague consistently failing to follow infection control protocols. What is the most appropriate action?

choices.text: Report the colleague to the supervisor immediately.
Talk to the colleague privately and offer guidance.
Document the observations and inform the unit manager.
Ignore the behavior; it's not the nurse's responsibility.

questions.rationale: Documenting observations and informing the unit manager is the appropriate first step.  Direct confrontation might be ineffective and could damage the working relationship.  Ignoring the behavior is unsafe and unprofessional.  Reporting immediately to the supervisor is premature without prior attempt at internal resolution.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse manager needs to delegate tasks effectively.  Which task is appropriate to delegate to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?

choices.text: Assessing a patient's neurological status post-stroke.
Monitoring a patient's intake and output.

questions.rationale: Monitoring I&O is within the scope of practice for an LPN.  Administering IV medications, changing central line dressings and performing a neurological assessment require the skills and judgment of an RN.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse just completed an initial assessment on four patients. Which patient requires the nurse’s immediate attention?

choices.text: A patient with a new onset of atrial fibrillation.
A patient with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) who is short of breath.
A patient with type 2 diabetes mellitus who is exhibiting signs of hyperglycemia.
A patient with a urinary tract infection (UTI) who is complaining of dysuria.

questions.rationale: New-onset atrial fibrillation is a serious arrhythmia that can lead to stroke or other life-threatening complications, requiring immediate attention. While the other patients require assessment and care, the risk associated with atrial fibrillation makes it the highest priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action is the best method to prevent the spread of infection?

choices.text: Wearing gloves when providing direct patient care.
Disposing of sharps in designated containers.

questions.rationale: Proper hand hygiene is the single most effective method to prevent the spread of infection. While the other options are also important infection control measures, hand hygiene is fundamental.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A newly hired nurse is struggling to manage her workload.  Which leadership style is MOST appropriate for the nurse manager?
questions.rationale: A democratic leadership style (C) empowers the nurse to participate in problem-solving and decision-making, fostering collaboration and ownership.  Autocratic (A) and laissez-faire (B) styles are less effective in this situation as they either provide insufficient support or lack direction.  While transformational (D) leadership is beneficial long-term, a more direct and supportive approach (C) is better suited to address the immediate issue.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A staff nurse is consistently late to work.  After multiple verbal warnings, which action is most appropriate for the nurse manager?

choices.text: Document the behavior and follow the progressive disciplinary process.
Assign the nurse to less demanding shifts.
Ignore the behavior, hoping it will improve.

questions.rationale: Following a progressive disciplinary process (B) is the appropriate approach to address consistent tardiness.  This ensures fairness and provides opportunities for improvement.  Immediate termination (A) is usually not the first step.  Assigning less demanding shifts (C) does not address the root cause of the problem.  Ignoring the behavior (D) is unprofessional and ineffective.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a urinary tract infection (UTI) reports burning during urination. Which nursing intervention is MOST important?
questions.rationale: The primary treatment for a UTI is antibiotics.  While the other interventions are important, administering antibiotics (B) is crucial to eradicate the infection and relieve symptoms.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which personal protective equipment (PPE) is required when caring for a patient with suspected tuberculosis?
questions.rationale: Tuberculosis is transmitted via airborne droplets, requiring an N95 respirator for protection.  Gloves and gown are also used to prevent contact transmission.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse just completed wound care for a patient with a MRSA infection. What is the next priority action?
questions.rationale: Hand hygiene is crucial to prevent the spread of infection immediately after any wound care, especially with a drug-resistant organism. Documentation (A) is important, but hand hygiene takes precedence.  PPE removal (B) is essential but follows hand hygiene.  Physician notification (D) may be needed, but hand hygiene prevents further transmission.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is on contact precautions for C. difficile. What PPE is required?
questions.rationale: Contact precautions require gown and gloves to prevent transmission of C. difficile through direct contact.  N95 masks (B, D) are for airborne precautions.  Eye protection (C) is not typically needed for C. difficile.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with tuberculosis (TB) is placed on airborne precautions.  What PPE is required?
questions.rationale: Airborne precautions require an N95 respirator to protect against the transmission of airborne pathogens like TB.  Gowns and gloves (A) are for contact precautions.  Surgical masks (C) are insufficient for airborne pathogens.  Eye protection (D) isn't typically necessary for TB.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action is the most effective method for preventing healthcare-associated infections?

choices.text: Using sterile gloves for all procedures.
Wearing a mask during patient contact.

questions.rationale: Frequent hand hygiene is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of healthcare-associated infections.  While other practices (A, B, D) are important, hand hygiene is the foundation of infection control.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A newly admitted patient requires frequent vital sign monitoring, medication administration, and wound care. Which tasks can be safely delegated to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?
questions.rationale: LPNs can administer oral medications (A) and monitor vital signs (B).  They can also change a sterile dressing (D) and document (F) the collected data.  Comprehensive wound assessments (C), head-to-toe assessments (E), and initial care planning require the higher-level skills of an RN.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action is most important to prevent falls in elderly patients?

choices.text: Keep the bed in the high position.
Use side rails on the bed.
Encourage patients to use assistive devices.

questions.rationale: Systematic fall risk assessment (C) allows for proactive interventions tailored to individual needs.  Keeping the bed high (A) and using side rails (B) can paradoxically increase fall risk.  Assistive devices (D) are helpful, but assessment guides appropriate usage.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task is most appropriate for the RN to delegate to the unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP)?
questions.rationale: Assisting with ambulation (C) is a task that can be safely delegated to the UAP.  Medication administration (A), pain assessment (B), and care planning (D) require the skills and judgment of an RN.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which precautions are necessary for a patient with tuberculosis (TB)?
questions.rationale: TB requires droplet (B) and airborne (C) precautions to prevent transmission through respiratory droplets and airborne particles.  Standard precautions (D) are always necessary.  Contact precautions (A) and protective isolation (E) are not indicated for TB.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task is most appropriate to delegate to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?

choices.text: Monitoring a patient's neurological status post-stroke.
Providing wound care for a stable patient.

questions.rationale: LPNs can perform many tasks, including providing wound care for a stable patient (D).  Administering IV medications (A) and developing nursing care plans (B) are typically within the scope of practice of registered nurses. Monitoring neurological status (C) requires more complex assessment skills.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving oxygen therapy via nasal cannula.  Which safety precaution is most important?

choices.text: Ensure the oxygen tank is properly secured.
Educate the patient about the use of oxygen.

questions.rationale: Oxygen tanks are under high pressure and pose a significant fire hazard. Ensuring the tank is properly secured (B) is the most important safety precaution to prevent accidents.  While the other options are important, they are secondary to safety.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a urinary tract infection (UTI) is prescribed antibiotics.  What should the nurse teach the patient?

choices.text: Stop taking antibiotics when symptoms improve.
Complete the entire course of antibiotics.
Take antibiotics only when experiencing pain.
Drink less fluid to reduce urinary frequency.

questions.rationale: Completing the entire course of antibiotics (B) ensures eradication of the infection and prevents antibiotic resistance.  The other options are incorrect.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task should the registered nurse (RN) NOT delegate to a certified nursing assistant (CNA)?
questions.rationale: Wound assessment (C) requires nursing judgment and is not within the scope of practice for a CNA.  The other tasks are appropriate to delegate.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action demonstrates ethical nursing practice?

choices.text: Advocating for a patient's right to refuse treatment.
Withholding information from the patient's family.

questions.rationale: Advocating for a patient's right to refuse treatment (B) is an ethical nursing responsibility.  The other options represent unethical practices.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse is caring for a patient with a diagnosed case of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). What type of precautions should the nurse implement?
questions.rationale: MRSA is spread through direct contact. Contact precautions (B) are necessary to prevent its transmission. Airborne (A), droplet (C), and standard (D) precautions are not sufficient to prevent MRSA spread.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse manager observes a staff nurse consistently failing to document patient care accurately. What is the most appropriate initial action?

choices.text: Immediately report the nurse to the hospital administration.
Provide the nurse with additional training on documentation.
Document the nurse's behavior in a performance appraisal.
Verbally counsel the nurse, clarifying expectations and providing support.

questions.rationale: Addressing the issue directly with the nurse (D) through verbal counseling is the most appropriate first step.  This allows for clarification, support, and identification of potential underlying reasons for the documentation errors. Reporting to administration (A) is premature. Additional training (B) may be necessary, but it's important to first address the issue directly with the nurse.  Documenting in a performance appraisal (C) is a later step if the problem persists after initial interventions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse is preparing to administer a medication. Which action demonstrates the highest level of safety?

choices.text: Checking the medication with another nurse.
Administering the medication before checking the patient's identification.
Verifying the medication order against the MAR.
Asking the patient if they have any allergies.

questions.rationale: Verifying the medication order against the MAR (C) ensures the correct medication, dose, and route are being administered.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse manager notices that several staff nurses are consistently arriving late for their shifts. What is the most effective approach?

choices.text: Immediately issue written warnings to all late nurses.
Hold a staff meeting to discuss the importance of punctuality.
Individually counsel each nurse to understand the reasons for their lateness.
Assign overtime to other nurses to cover for the late nurses.

questions.rationale: Individually counseling each nurse (C) allows for understanding the underlying reasons for lateness and developing tailored solutions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which of the following are standard precautions used to prevent the spread of infection?
questions.rationale: Standard precautions apply to all patients regardless of their diagnosis.  Airborne and contact precautions are used in addition to standard precautions for specific infections. Sterile technique is not always required.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient reports a pain level of 8/10 after surgery.  Which of the following is the most appropriate initial action?

choices.text: Reassess the patient’s pain in 30 minutes.
Teach the patient about nonpharmacological pain management techniques.
Tell the patient to try to tough it out.

questions.rationale: Given the high pain level, administering the prescribed analgesic is the most appropriate initial action.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Before administering any medication, the nurse should:

choices.text: Ask the patient their name and date of birth.
Verify the medication order with the MAR.

questions.rationale: All three actions are essential steps in medication administration to ensure patient safety.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse should use which of the following devices to prevent falls in a confused patient?
questions.rationale: All are appropriate depending on the patient's needs and mobility status.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse observes a colleague making a medication error.  What is the appropriate action?

choices.text: Report the error to the appropriate supervisor.
Confront the colleague directly in front of other staff.
Wait until the end of the shift to report the error privately.

questions.rationale: Reporting the error promptly and through proper channels ensures patient safety.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Standard precautions mandate the use of which protective equipment when caring for a patient with suspected influenza?
questions.rationale: Standard precautions always include gloves and eye protection when there's a risk of body fluid exposure.  A gown might be necessary depending on the level of anticipated contact with bodily fluids, but it's not always required for influenza.  An N95 respirator is needed for airborne precautions, which would be implemented in addition to standard precautions for patients with confirmed airborne diseases. Shoe covers are generally not part of standard precautions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Before administering medications, what is the nurse's most important action?
questions.rationale: Verifying the medication order against the MAR is the most critical step to ensure the right medication is given.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is at risk for falls.  Which intervention is most effective?

choices.text: Place the bed in the lowest position
Keep the call light within reach

questions.rationale: All of the listed options are effective fall prevention strategies.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Standard precautions for preventing the spread of infection include:

choices.text: Wearing gloves when handling bodily fluids.
Wearing a gown when potential for splashing body fluids.
Wearing an N95 mask for all patient interactions.
Disposing of sharps in designated containers.

questions.rationale: Standard precautions apply to all patients regardless of their diagnosis.  Options A, B, C, and E are crucial components of standard precautions.  Option D is incorrect; N95 masks are used for airborne precautions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient newly diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus needs education on managing their condition. Which teaching point is MOST crucial for immediate patient safety?

choices.text: Understanding the long-term complications of diabetes.

questions.rationale: Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) is a life-threatening complication of diabetes that requires immediate attention.  While all options are important, recognizing and treating hypoglycemia is paramount for immediate patient safety.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Before administering any medication, what is the MOST important action a nurse should take?
questions.rationale: Verifying the medication order (the "five rights" of medication administration) is paramount to preventing medication errors.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The patient reports pain as a 7/10 on a numerical rating scale.  What is the nurse's next step?

choices.text: Assess the characteristics of the pain.

questions.rationale: A comprehensive pain assessment is crucial to determine the best course of action for pain management.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with Clostridium difficile infection (CDI) is being cared for. Which of the following precautions are necessary?
questions.rationale: CDI is spread via the fecal-oral route, requiring contact precautions. Standard precautions are always necessary.  Gowns and gloves are essential for contact precautions. Droplet and airborne precautions are not indicated for CDI.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving oxygen therapy via nasal cannula. What safety precaution is crucial?

choices.text: Ensure the oxygen tank is secured upright.
Check for skin breakdown around the cannula prongs.
Avoid using petroleum-based products near oxygen.

questions.rationale: All options are crucial safety precautions when administering oxygen therapy.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Standard precautions should be used for which patient?

choices.text: A patient with methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA).

questions.rationale: Standard precautions apply to all patients to prevent the spread of infection.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is at risk for falls. What is the most important nursing intervention?

choices.text: Place a bed alarm on the bed.
Assess the patient's fall risk factors.
Educate the family on fall prevention.

questions.rationale: A thorough fall risk assessment is the foundation for developing a personalized fall prevention plan.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with Clostridium difficile infection (CDI) is on contact precautions.  Which nursing actions are appropriate?

choices.text: Place the patient in a private room.
Use soap and water hand hygiene.
Dispose of soiled linens in a designated bag.

questions.rationale: CDI requires contact precautions, including private rooms, gowns and gloves, and thorough hand hygiene with soap and water (alcohol-based rubs are insufficient for C. diff). Soiled linens should be disposed of appropriately.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A newly hired nurse consistently arrives late to work.  What is the best initial approach?

choices.text: Have a private conversation to understand the reason for tardiness.
Ignore the behavior and hope it improves.

questions.rationale: Before disciplinary actions, it's crucial to address the issue directly and privately, giving the nurse an opportunity to explain the situation.  This allows for a more supportive and constructive approach.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is being discharged home on oxygen. What is crucial patient teaching?
questions.rationale: All listed precautions are crucial for safe oxygen use at home to prevent fire hazards.

Bed rails (D) can provide additional stability, but should not be used if they pose a risk to the patient.  Educating the patient (F) empowers them to participate in their own safety.  Administering sedatives (E) increases the fall risk.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task is MOST appropriate for the RN to delegate to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?

choices.text: Monitoring a patient's neurological status post-stroke.
Changing a sterile dressing on a surgical wound.

questions.rationale: LPNs can perform tasks such as changing sterile dressings (D) under the supervision of an RN.  Administering IV medications (A), developing care plans (B), and monitoring complex neurological status (C) require the advanced skills and judgment of an RN.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A newly hired nurse is consistently late for work and makes frequent charting errors. Which action should the nurse manager take first?

choices.text: Document the issues and submit a performance improvement plan (PIP).
Report the nurse to the state board of nursing.

questions.rationale: Progressive discipline is essential.  Verbal counseling and coaching provide an opportunity for the nurse to improve before more formal actions are taken.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving oxygen therapy via nasal cannula. Which safety precaution is essential?

choices.text: Keep flammable materials away from the patient.
Ensure the patient's oxygen saturation is above 95%.
Monitor the patient for signs of hypoxemia.

questions.rationale: Oxygen is flammable, and precautions must be taken to prevent fire hazards.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which type of isolation precaution is appropriate for a patient with tuberculosis?
questions.rationale: Tuberculosis is transmitted through airborne droplets.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with chronic back pain reports increased pain despite taking prescribed analgesics. What non-pharmacological pain management strategies should the nurse suggest?
questions.rationale: Multiple non-pharmacological methods can be used to manage chronic pain.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action best demonstrates the principles of infection control in a community setting?

choices.text: Wearing a mask while visiting a patient with influenza.
Properly disposing of sharps in designated containers.
Using gloves when handling bodily fluids.

questions.rationale: Frequent and thorough handwashing (C) is the most fundamental and effective infection control measure in all settings, including community health.  While the other options are also important, handwashing is the cornerstone of preventing the spread of infections.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task is MOST appropriate for the RN to delegate to a Licensed Practical Nurse (LPN)?

choices.text: Monitoring a patient's response to medication.

questions.rationale: LPNs can monitor a patient's response to medication (D) and report changes to the RN.  Administering IV medications (A), assessing a new patient (B), and developing a care plan (C) are within the RN's scope of practice.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with suspected tuberculosis (TB) is admitted. Which precautions are necessary?
questions.rationale: TB requires airborne precautions (D) to prevent the spread of airborne pathogens.  Standard precautions (A) are always necessary to prevent transmission of microorganisms from all patients.  Contact (B), droplet (C), and protective isolation (E) are not indicated in TB cases.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse is caring for four patients: one with acute respiratory distress, one with chest pain, one with a new-onset seizure, and one with post-surgical pain. Which patient requires the nurse's immediate attention?

choices.text: The patient with acute respiratory distress.
The patient with a new-onset seizure.

questions.rationale: Acute respiratory distress (A) is a life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention to prevent respiratory failure. While the other patients need care, the patient with acute respiratory distress requires the most immediate attention.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which precautions are necessary when caring for a patient with Clostridium difficile (C. diff)?

choices.text: Use of soap and water for hand hygiene

questions.rationale: C. difficile is spread through contact with spores.  Contact precautions are essential.  Soap and water are necessary to remove the spores, alcohol-based hand rubs are not effective.  Gown and gloves are part of contact precautions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse manager needs to delegate tasks effectively.  Which task is appropriate to delegate to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?

choices.text: Assessing a patient's neurological status post-stroke.
Monitoring a patient's intake and output.

questions.rationale: Monitoring I&O is within the scope of practice for an LPN.  Administering IV medications, changing central line dressings and performing a neurological assessment require the skills and judgment of an RN.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse observes a colleague consistently failing to follow infection control protocols. What is the most appropriate action?

choices.text: Report the colleague to the supervisor immediately.
Talk to the colleague privately and offer guidance.
Document the observations and inform the unit manager.
Ignore the behavior; it's not the nurse's responsibility.

questions.rationale: Documenting observations and informing the unit manager is the appropriate first step.  Direct confrontation might be ineffective and could damage the working relationship.  Ignoring the behavior is unsafe and unprofessional.  Reporting immediately to the supervisor is premature without prior attempt at internal resolution.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse just completed an initial assessment on four patients. Which patient requires the nurse’s immediate attention?

choices.text: A patient with a new onset of atrial fibrillation.
A patient with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) who is short of breath.
A patient with type 2 diabetes mellitus who is exhibiting signs of hyperglycemia.
A patient with a urinary tract infection (UTI) who is complaining of dysuria.

questions.rationale: New-onset atrial fibrillation is a serious arrhythmia that can lead to stroke or other life-threatening complications, requiring immediate attention. While the other patients require assessment and care, the risk associated with atrial fibrillation makes it the highest priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action is the best method to prevent the spread of infection?

choices.text: Wearing gloves when providing direct patient care.
Disposing of sharps in designated containers.

questions.rationale: Proper hand hygiene is the single most effective method to prevent the spread of infection. While the other options are also important infection control measures, hand hygiene is fundamental.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A newly hired nurse is struggling to manage her workload.  Which leadership style is MOST appropriate for the nurse manager?
questions.rationale: A democratic leadership style (C) empowers the nurse to participate in problem-solving and decision-making, fostering collaboration and ownership.  Autocratic (A) and laissez-faire (B) styles are less effective in this situation as they either provide insufficient support or lack direction.  While transformational (D) leadership is beneficial long-term, a more direct and supportive approach (C) is better suited to address the immediate issue.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A staff nurse is consistently late to work.  After multiple verbal warnings, which action is most appropriate for the nurse manager?

choices.text: Document the behavior and follow the progressive disciplinary process.
Assign the nurse to less demanding shifts.
Ignore the behavior, hoping it will improve.

questions.rationale: Following a progressive disciplinary process (B) is the appropriate approach to address consistent tardiness.  This ensures fairness and provides opportunities for improvement.  Immediate termination (A) is usually not the first step.  Assigning less demanding shifts (C) does not address the root cause of the problem.  Ignoring the behavior (D) is unprofessional and ineffective.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a urinary tract infection (UTI) reports burning during urination. Which nursing intervention is MOST important?
questions.rationale: The primary treatment for a UTI is antibiotics.  While the other interventions are important, administering antibiotics (B) is crucial to eradicate the infection and relieve symptoms.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which personal protective equipment (PPE) is required when caring for a patient with suspected tuberculosis?
questions.rationale: Tuberculosis is transmitted via airborne droplets, requiring an N95 respirator for protection.  Gloves and gown are also used to prevent contact transmission.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse just completed wound care for a patient with a MRSA infection. What is the next priority action?
questions.rationale: Hand hygiene is crucial to prevent the spread of infection immediately after any wound care, especially with a drug-resistant organism. Documentation (A) is important, but hand hygiene takes precedence.  PPE removal (B) is essential but follows hand hygiene.  Physician notification (D) may be needed, but hand hygiene prevents further transmission.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is on contact precautions for C. difficile. What PPE is required?
questions.rationale: Contact precautions require gown and gloves to prevent transmission of C. difficile through direct contact.  N95 masks (B, D) are for airborne precautions.  Eye protection (C) is not typically needed for C. difficile.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with tuberculosis (TB) is placed on airborne precautions.  What PPE is required?
questions.rationale: Airborne precautions require an N95 respirator to protect against the transmission of airborne pathogens like TB.  Gowns and gloves (A) are for contact precautions.  Surgical masks (C) are insufficient for airborne pathogens.  Eye protection (D) isn't typically necessary for TB.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action is the most effective method for preventing healthcare-associated infections?

choices.text: Using sterile gloves for all procedures.
Wearing a mask during patient contact.

questions.rationale: Frequent hand hygiene is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of healthcare-associated infections.  While other practices (A, B, D) are important, hand hygiene is the foundation of infection control.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A newly admitted patient requires frequent vital sign monitoring, medication administration, and wound care. Which tasks can be safely delegated to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?
questions.rationale: LPNs can administer oral medications (A) and monitor vital signs (B).  They can also change a sterile dressing (D) and document (F) the collected data.  Comprehensive wound assessments (C), head-to-toe assessments (E), and initial care planning require the higher-level skills of an RN.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action is most important to prevent falls in elderly patients?

choices.text: Keep the bed in the high position.
Use side rails on the bed.
Encourage patients to use assistive devices.

questions.rationale: Systematic fall risk assessment (C) allows for proactive interventions tailored to individual needs.  Keeping the bed high (A) and using side rails (B) can paradoxically increase fall risk.  Assistive devices (D) are helpful, but assessment guides appropriate usage.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task is most appropriate for the RN to delegate to the unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP)?
questions.rationale: Assisting with ambulation (C) is a task that can be safely delegated to the UAP.  Medication administration (A), pain assessment (B), and care planning (D) require the skills and judgment of an RN.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which precautions are necessary for a patient with tuberculosis (TB)?
questions.rationale: TB requires droplet (B) and airborne (C) precautions to prevent transmission through respiratory droplets and airborne particles.  Standard precautions (D) are always necessary.  Contact precautions (A) and protective isolation (E) are not indicated for TB.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task is most appropriate to delegate to a licensed practical nurse (LPN)?

choices.text: Monitoring a patient's neurological status post-stroke.
Providing wound care for a stable patient.

questions.rationale: LPNs can perform many tasks, including providing wound care for a stable patient (D).  Administering IV medications (A) and developing nursing care plans (B) are typically within the scope of practice of registered nurses. Monitoring neurological status (C) requires more complex assessment skills.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is receiving oxygen therapy via nasal cannula.  Which safety precaution is most important?

choices.text: Ensure the oxygen tank is properly secured.
Educate the patient about the use of oxygen.

questions.rationale: Oxygen tanks are under high pressure and pose a significant fire hazard. Ensuring the tank is properly secured (B) is the most important safety precaution to prevent accidents.  While the other options are important, they are secondary to safety.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with a urinary tract infection (UTI) is prescribed antibiotics.  What should the nurse teach the patient?

choices.text: Stop taking antibiotics when symptoms improve.
Complete the entire course of antibiotics.
Take antibiotics only when experiencing pain.
Drink less fluid to reduce urinary frequency.

questions.rationale: Completing the entire course of antibiotics (B) ensures eradication of the infection and prevents antibiotic resistance.  The other options are incorrect.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which task should the registered nurse (RN) NOT delegate to a certified nursing assistant (CNA)?
questions.rationale: Wound assessment (C) requires nursing judgment and is not within the scope of practice for a CNA.  The other tasks are appropriate to delegate.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which action demonstrates ethical nursing practice?

choices.text: Advocating for a patient's right to refuse treatment.
Withholding information from the patient's family.

questions.rationale: Advocating for a patient's right to refuse treatment (B) is an ethical nursing responsibility.  The other options represent unethical practices.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse is caring for a patient with a diagnosed case of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). What type of precautions should the nurse implement?
questions.rationale: MRSA is spread through direct contact. Contact precautions (B) are necessary to prevent its transmission. Airborne (A), droplet (C), and standard (D) precautions are not sufficient to prevent MRSA spread.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse manager observes a staff nurse consistently failing to document patient care accurately. What is the most appropriate initial action?

choices.text: Immediately report the nurse to the hospital administration.
Provide the nurse with additional training on documentation.
Document the nurse's behavior in a performance appraisal.
Verbally counsel the nurse, clarifying expectations and providing support.

questions.rationale: Addressing the issue directly with the nurse (D) through verbal counseling is the most appropriate first step.  This allows for clarification, support, and identification of potential underlying reasons for the documentation errors. Reporting to administration (A) is premature. Additional training (B) may be necessary, but it's important to first address the issue directly with the nurse.  Documenting in a performance appraisal (C) is a later step if the problem persists after initial interventions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: The nurse is preparing to administer a medication. Which action demonstrates the highest level of safety?

choices.text: Checking the medication with another nurse.
Administering the medication before checking the patient's identification.
Verifying the medication order against the MAR.
Asking the patient if they have any allergies.

questions.rationale: Verifying the medication order against the MAR (C) ensures the correct medication, dose, and route are being administered.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A nurse manager notices that several staff nurses are consistently arriving late for their shifts. What is the most effective approach?

choices.text: Immediately issue written warnings to all late nurses.
Hold a staff meeting to discuss the importance of punctuality.
Individually counsel each nurse to understand the reasons for their lateness.
Assign overtime to other nurses to cover for the late nurses.

questions.rationale: Individually counseling each nurse (C) allows for understanding the underlying reasons for lateness and developing tailored solutions.
